price_momentum_12_1.name: price_momentum_12_1
price_momentum_12_1.rationale: Stocks that have outperformed over the past year tend to keep outperforming over the next few months as investors underreact to information; the most recent month is skipped to avoid short-term reversal contamination.
price_momentum_12_1.source_title: Returns to Buying Winners and Selling Losers: Implications for Stock Market Efficiency (Jegadeesh & Titman, 1993)

high_52week_proximity.name: high_52week_proximity
high_52week_proximity.rationale: Nearness to the 52-week high acts as an anchor: when a stock approaches its yearly high, traders are reluctant to bid it higher, causing underreaction, so stocks near their highs subsequently outperform those far below.
high_52week_proximity.source_title: The 52-Week High and Momentum Investing (George & Hwang, 2004)

moving_average_trend.name: moving_average_trend
moving_average_trend.rationale: Simple trend-following rules (price above a long moving average) capture persistent drift; stocks in established uptrends tend to continue, a relationship documented across decades of index data.
moving_average_trend.source_title: Simple Technical Trading Rules and the Stochastic Properties of Stock Returns (Brock, Lakonishok & LeBaron, 1992)

short_term_reversal.name: short_term_reversal
short_term_reversal.rationale: Over short horizons (about one month) individual stock returns exhibit negative serial correlation, partly from liquidity provision and overreaction, so last month's losers tend to bounce and winners tend to give back gains.
short_term_reversal.source_title: Evidence of Predictable Behavior of Security Returns (Jegadeesh, 1990)

residual_reversal.name: residual_reversal
residual_reversal.rationale: Reversal is far stronger when measured on the portion of returns unrelated to common factors: the idiosyncratic (residual) component reverses because it largely reflects temporary, non-fundamental price pressure rather than news.
residual_reversal.source_title: A Closer Look at the Short-Term Return Reversal (Da, Liu & Schaumburg)

weekly_max_reversal.name: weekly_max_reversal
weekly_max_reversal.rationale: Short-term reversal is concentrated in stocks that recently printed extreme single-day gains (lottery-like spikes); such stocks are overbought by attention-driven traders and subsequently reverse hard at the weekly horizon.
weekly_max_reversal.source_title: Maxing Out Short-term Reversals in Weekly Stock Returns (Journal of Empirical Finance, 2025)

amihud_illiquidity.name: amihud_illiquidity
amihud_illiquidity.rationale: Illiquid stocks, where a given dollar of trading moves the price more, command a return premium as compensation for higher trading costs and price-impact risk.
amihud_illiquidity.source_title: Illiquidity and Stock Returns: Cross-Section and Time-Series Effects (Amihud, 2002)

liquidity_dollar_volume.name: liquidity_dollar_volume
liquidity_dollar_volume.rationale: Trading activity proxies for liquidity: more liquid, heavily traded stocks earn lower expected returns, so low-liquidity names carry a premium. With only OHLCV available, average daily dollar volume is the natural liquidity proxy for the turnover effect.
liquidity_dollar_volume.source_title: Liquidity and Stock Returns: An Alternative Test (Datar, Naik & Radcliffe, 1998)

abnormal_volume.name: abnormal_volume
abnormal_volume.rationale: Spikes in trading volume relative to a stock's own norm signal shifts in investor attention and information flow; abnormal volume predicts the cross-section of subsequent returns beyond past-return effects.
abnormal_volume.source_title: Abnormal Trading Volume and the Cross-Section of Stock Returns (EFMA, 2016)

volume_price_trend.name: volume_price_trend
volume_price_trend.rationale: Volume confirms price: accumulating signed volume (volume attributed in the direction of the day's price change) reveals underlying buying or selling pressure before it is fully reflected in price.
volume_price_trend.source_title: Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) / On-Balance Volume - Investopedia reference

idiosyncratic_vol.name: idiosyncratic_vol
idiosyncratic_vol.rationale: Stocks with high idiosyncratic (firm-specific) volatility earn abnormally low future returns, an anomaly often attributed to mispricing, lottery preferences, and limits to arbitrage.
idiosyncratic_vol.source_title: The Cross-Section of Volatility and Expected Returns (Ang, Hodrick, Xing & Zhang, 2006)

low_total_vol.name: low_total_vol
low_total_vol.rationale: The low-volatility anomaly: stocks with low total return volatility deliver higher risk-adjusted returns than high-volatility stocks, contradicting the textbook risk-return tradeoff.
low_total_vol.source_title: The Volatility Effect: Lower Risk Without Lower Return (Blitz & van Vliet, 2007)

max_lottery.name: max_lottery
max_lottery.rationale: Investors overpay for lottery-like stocks with extreme recent single-day upside, pushing their prices too high; these high-MAX stocks subsequently underperform.
max_lottery.source_title: Maxing Out: Stocks as Lotteries and the Cross-Section of Expected Returns (Bali, Cakici & Whitelaw, 2011)

corwin_schultz_spread.name: corwin_schultz_spread
corwin_schultz_spread.rationale: Daily high prices are typically buyer-initiated and lows seller-initiated, so the ratio of high to low embeds the bid-ask spread; wider implied spreads proxy illiquidity and command a return premium.
corwin_schultz_spread.source_title: A Simple Way to Estimate Bid-Ask Spreads from Daily High and Low Prices (Corwin & Schultz, 2012)

roll_effective_spread.name: roll_effective_spread
roll_effective_spread.rationale: In an efficient market the bid-ask bounce induces negative serial covariance in successive price changes; the magnitude of that covariance implies the effective spread, a liquidity/transaction-cost proxy that is priced in the cross-section.
roll_effective_spread.source_title: A Simple Implicit Measure of the Effective Bid-Ask Spread in an Efficient Market (Roll, 1984)

overnight_intraday.name: overnight_intraday
overnight_intraday.rationale: Anomaly returns accrue very differently overnight versus intraday: overnight (close-to-open) returns are dominated by retail/sentiment-driven demand and persist, while intraday returns behave differently, so decomposing the two predicts the cross-section.
overnight_intraday.source_title: The Cross-Section of Intraday and Overnight Returns (Bogousslavsky, 2021)

intraday_close_strength.name: intraday_close_strength
intraday_close_strength.rationale: The position of the close within the day's high-low range, and the close-versus-open move, capture intraday demand pressure; this is the basis of several short-horizon formulaic alphas built purely from OHLC bars.

long_term_reversal.name: long_term_reversal
long_term_reversal.rationale: Over multi-year horizons investors overreact to sustained good or bad news, so past long-run losers (which become cheap/value-like) outperform past long-run winners as prices mean-revert toward fundamentals.
long_term_reversal.source_title: Does the Stock Market Overreact? (De Bondt & Thaler, 1985)

month_of_year_seasonality.name: month_of_year_seasonality
month_of_year_seasonality.rationale: Stocks that historically earn relatively high returns in a given calendar month tend to keep earning high returns in that same month for years, a persistent cross-sectional seasonal pattern.
month_of_year_seasonality.source_title: Seasonality in the Cross-Section of Stock Returns (Heston & Sadka, 2008)

day_of_week_seasonality.name: day_of_week_seasonality
day_of_week_seasonality.rationale: Long-short anomaly and speculative-stock returns vary systematically with the day of the week (e.g. sentiment-driven legs do better on certain weekdays), creating exploitable day-of-week patterns in the cross-section.
day_of_week_seasonality.source_title: Day of the Week and the Cross-Section of Returns (Birru)
